reasoning.typography: Strong typographic system with confident scale on the hero ('Build better sites, faster'), but falls into the rounded geometric sans trap that's become ubiquitous in 2024-2026 SaaS
reasoning.hierarchy: Hero dominates effectively with massive type, but the gallery wall below competes for attention rather than supporting a single message per scroll depth
reasoning.distinctiveness: Classic SaaS homepage stencil: hero statement + social proof name-drop ('Fortune 500') + dual CTA buttons + customer gallery. Swap 'Framer' for 'Webflow' and it still works
reasoning.color: Black background with white type is disciplined, but the multicolor gallery breaks restraint — every customer thumbnail screams for attention simultaneously
reasoning.density: Hero has confident breathing room, but transitions abruptly to a dense undifferentiated gallery wall. No rhythm between 'generous' and 'considered' — just binary extremes
reasoning.voice: Generic builder positioning: 'trusted by leading startups' / 'build fast and scale' / 'Start for free' — interchangeable with any site builder. No actual story about *why* or *how* Framer is different

winning_patterns: Hero typography scale shows real confidence — the headline does visual work
Black canvas creates strong container for the brand mark and navigation
Restrained top-level hierarchy before the gallery
anti_patterns: Gallery wall as primary content strategy — quantity over curation
Dual CTA pattern ('Start for free' + 'Start with AI') — classic conversion optimization stencil
Social proof by association ('Fortune 500') rather than showing actual work
No structural voice — could be any site builder's homepage
State of Sites report banner feels tacked-on rather than integrated
_note: Heavy motion, animated hero, scroll-driven choreography
